description: A 'punoki' is an organised social arrangement that continues beyond any one participant or meeting. Its roles, practices, and records let it persist when people change. 'punoa' (society) is the wider social fabric; an institution is one maintained arrangement within it.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release into high-back rounded 'u'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the sound through the nose, and hold stressed true-mid rounded 'o'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and finish on high-front 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The rounded enclosures of 'pu' and stressed 'no' echo the opening of 'punoa' (society). Final 'ki' adds a small, crisp contact. The broader social resonance ends at a more definite edge.
usage_notes: A 'punoki' is countable. A council may be one institution, but a meeting is only an event in its life. The surrounding account identifies its people and roles, along with the practices or resources that keep it operating. Claimed authority belongs in that account; 'kirethu' (legitimate) remains a separate judgement.

pillars.solarpunk-values: A durable commons needs arrangements that keep records and distribute work after the founding enthusiasm has gone home for supper.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Calling an arrangement an institution grants it no legitimacy, which leaves its authority and effects open to examination.

semantic_domains.community: an organised social arrangement maintained across changes of participant
